nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, pond margins, seasonally flooded wetlands, and slow-moving shallow waters across a broad subtropical to tropical range

summary: The Pygmy Chain Sword (Helanthium tenellum) is a small, grass-like foreground plant from the Americas, widely regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly carpeting species available. It spreads by runners to form a dense, lawn-like mat across the substrate.
careNotes: The Pygmy Chain Sword, known scientifically as Helanthium tenellum, is a small rosette plant generally regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly carpeting species in the freshwater hobby. The older scientific name Echinodorus tenellus is still widely used in the aquarium trade. Mature leaves are commonly reported in the 5–10 cm range, though stems can stretch taller under lower light. A green-leaf cultivar is noted for staying fresh green even under intense lighting, in contrast with reddish varieties. A nutrient-rich substrate is generally advised, and root tabs tend to help establish a thick carpet. Light demand is commonly described as medium to high — the plant will survive under lower light but tends to grow taller and less compact. CO2 injection is optional, but supplemental CO2 (around 20–40 mg/l) and balanced macro- and micro-nutrient fertilization tend to produce the densest carpets most quickly. The species tolerates a broad temperature range, with 18–28 °C generally cited as the comfortable band. Its native range extends across the eastern United States (from Texas and Florida north to Michigan and Massachusetts), southern Mexico, the West Indies (Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola), and South America south to Argentina; it is conservation-listed in several US states. The aquarium trade commonly distinguishes a Narrow Leaf Chain Sword form, with slender grass-like blades, from a Broad Leaf Chain Sword form with wider strap-like leaves and a fuller appearance. The broad-leaf form is sometimes sold under the older name Echinodorus quadricostatus or as the Dwarf Swordplant. Several taxonomic treatments fold E. quadricostatus into the broadly variable Helanthium tenellum complex, and the trade generally treats them as cultivar-grade variants of the same chain-sword carpeting plant. The broad-leaf form is reported tolerant of a similar 20–29 °C range, prefers moderate to bright light, benefits from CO2 in high-light tanks, and propagates by runners in identical fashion.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily by sending out runners (stolons) that produce new daughter rosettes along the substrate, forming a chain - hence the common name 'Pygmy Chain Sword'. Has been reported to flower when grown emersed.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temp 18-28°C optimal, pH 5-7.5, kH 0-14 dKH), CO2 (20-40 mg/l), height (6-10 cm), growth rate (fast), propagation (runners), and foreground placement.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Alismataceae / Alismatales), former scientific name Echinodorus tenellus, native range across the eastern US, southern Mexico, West Indies, Central and South America to Argentina, and propagation by runners.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Source for the 'Chain Sword Broad Leaf' trade variant (merged into this entry per playbook 4c). Page URL slugs the species as Echinodorus tenellus but the product description identifies it as Echinodorus quadricostatus (Dwarf Swordplant), distinguished from the 'Narrow Leaf variety' by wider strap-like leaves. Confirmed care: temperature 68-84°F (20-29°C), moderate-bright lighting, CO2 optional/beneficial, foreground placement, runner propagation, nutrient-rich substrate. Native range stated as Brazil. Taxonomic relationship between E. quadricostatus and H. tenellum is unresolved in cited sources - merged here as a trade-level variant pending firmer authority.
